text: A fast converter with one zone and an internal breeding ratio of 1.00, with liquid fuel in the form of molten plutonium - uranium - and sodium chloride, with a thermal power of 3 GW (th) allows continuous extraction of the volatile fission products (Br, I, Kr, Xe, Te) by means of helium purging in the core.
The impact on an accidental release of fission products is rather significant; the amounts released are 50-100 times smaller than those in a reference reactor (LWR with oxide fuel).

Because the heat sink is relatively large and after heat reduced, the temperature of the fuel does not exceed $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ after an accident, which greatly reduces the consequences of an accident.

Ein Schneller konverter Reaktor mit einer Zone und einem Bruffaktor von 1.00, mit flüssigem Brennstoff in Form von geschmolzenem Plutonium -, Uranium - und Natriumchlorid, mit einer Leistung von 3 GW (th) erlaubt die kontinuierliche Enfernung von flüchtigen Spaltprodukten (Br, I, Kr, Xe, Te) durch Durchleiten von Helium im Core.

Die Folgen bei einer unfallbedingten Freigabe von Spaltprodukten sindrecht signifikant; die freiigegebenen Mengen sind 50-100 mal kleiner als die beim Referenzreaktor (einem LWR mit Oxiden als Brennstoff).

Weil die Wärmesenke relativ gross ist, und die Nachwärme reduziert ist, übersteigt die Temperatur des Brennstoffs nach einem Unfall $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ nicht, was zu einer bedeutenden Reduktion der Unfallfolgen führt.
1.4 The present State of Reactor Development and the Criteria for a Safer Reactor.
The best reason for such a reactor type has been given by Alvin Weinberg (1979, 1980).
"For the 15 billion curies contained in a 1000 megawatt reactor we could never say that the chance of a serious accidental release was zero.

For nuclear energy to survive we must reduce the probability of any serious malfunction much below the 1 in 20 000 per reactor-year estimated in the Rasmussen report as well as reducing any possible consequences."

"But if the world energy system involved as many as 5000 reactors - that is, 10 times as many as are now either in operation or under construction - one might expect an accident that released sizeable amounts of radioactivity every four years.
If a man is to live with fission over the long term he must reduce the a priori probability of accident by a large factor - say 100."

The aim of this paper is to try and present a concept for a fission power reactor being approximately 100 times less hazardous than existing reactor types, and with much less than 15 billion curies activity.
How is it possible to meet the requirement for a 100-fold improvement in reactor safety?

a) the amount of fission products in the core during a normal operation must be reduced by a factor 100 which significantly reduces the effects of a large accident where the fission products would be released into the environment,
b) non-volatile F.P.2) Decay heat removalImmediately after shutdown ~180 MW(th).
The integrated decay heat over some hours is of the order of 1000 GigajoulesMinimum decay heat.
At least by more than one order of magnitude3) Pressure in fuel and coolant (in containment)a)~150 bar in a PWR ~80 bar in a BWR in the coolant.
In a liquid metal cooled reactor an exothermic reaction is possible $4\mathrm{Na}_{\mathrm{met}} + \mathrm{UO}_2 + 2\mathrm{Na}_2\mathrm{O} + \mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{met}}$
A self sufficient reactor with a breeding ratio of $\sim 1$ has no external circulation of Pu
a) thermal breeder with molten fluorides developed decades ago by Oak Ridge National Laboratory, existing as an experimental reactor with a power of 8 MW(th), (Ref.

1.4 The present State of Reactor Development and the Criteria for a Safer Reactor
1.1) the present state of reactor development.

a) the most important parameter to be optimised is the reduction in the inventory of volatile fission products (which will control the consequences of an accident during the first hours) and fission products such as Sr-90 and Cs-137 (which determine the accessibility of the contaminated area for tens of years),
c) the breeding ratio has been set at 1 in order to minimise the need to transport fissionable material. this makes the reactor a net zero consumer and zero producer of fissionable material,

With a time constant of extraction $t_{\text{ext}}$ arbitrarily chosen as $10^{2}$ s the following results can be obtained:

In a 3 GW(th) reactor $9.3 \times 10^{19}$ fission/s occur producing $3.09 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{s}$ of fission products, $1 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{s}$ of which are volatile.

The total volume of helium bubbles in the core should not exceed $0.1 \mathrm{~m}^{3}$ (less than $0.2\%$ of the total core volume) that is 5 moles of helium in order to minimise the rise in criticality due to loss of helium throughput.
Therefore to a first approximation the average rate of mass transfer across the interface $(N_{A})_{avg}$ is: (Ref.2)
\left(\mathrm {N} _ {\mathrm {A}}\right) _ {\text {a v g}} \quad = \frac {4 \theta_ {\mathrm {A B}}}{\pi \cdot t _ {\text {c o n t}}} \cdot {} ^ {\mathrm {C}} _ {\mathrm {A O}}
A uniform bubble diameter of $3\mathrm{mm}$ was assumed and $\theta_{\mathrm{AB}}$ calculated from the Stokes-Einstein equation.

For a typical volatile molecule with a molecular weight of $\sim 30$ the value of $\theta_{\mathrm{AB}}$ is $\sim 2\mathrm{x}10^{-9}\mathrm{m}^2/\mathrm{s}$ . $C_{\mathrm{Ho}}$ thus equals $7\mathrm{x}10^{-6}\mathrm{mol}/\mathrm{m}^3$ equivalent to the production for 7 seconds.

d) under normal operation the core contains $\sim 0.2\%$ of helium bubbles.
A depressurization of the fuel system which would expand the bubbles by a factor 2 to 3 would cause a reactivity decrease,
The rather simple core catcher in the form of $\sim 2000$ tons of salt (see Fig.
3.2) is sufficient to control this accident,
- removal of the decay heat is guaranteed even in case of a failure of all emergency cooling systems provided the outside containment spraying system works, for a power of less than 0.5 MW.
